html:
Lars Olof Jonathan (Nathan) Söderblom, born January 15, 1866, in Trönö parsonage, Hälsingland, and died July 12, 1931, in Uppsala, was a Swedish theologian and religious historian who served as the Archbishop of the Church of Sweden from 1914 to 1931. He became a member of the Swedish Academy in 1921 and received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1930 for his ecumenical peace work.

== Biography ==

Nathan Söderblom was born in Trönö parsonage in the parish of Norrala, and grew up in the parsonage in Bjuråker as the son of Jonas Söderblom (born 1823 in Söderala, died 1901), who later became a pastor in Norrala, and his wife, Nikolina Sophie Blûme (born 1838 in Strömstad, died 1913 in Uppsala), who came from Denmark. The Söderblom family originates from Söderala, and the name was adopted by Nathan Söderblom's father and uncles. His paternal grandfather, Jon Olsson, was a church warden and member of parliament for Orsta nr 3. His childhood was marked by his father's strong religious beliefs, influenced in part by Rosenius and the revival movement. After graduating from high school in Hudiksvall in 1883, Nathan Söderblom enrolled at Uppsala University and earned a Bachelor of Arts degree in 1886. Initially, he hesitated about whether to pursue classical philology, but eventually decided to become a priest. In 1892, after a trip to the United States, he obtained a Bachelor of Theology degree and was ordained as a priest the following year by Gottfrid Billing. His first position as a priest was at Uppsala Hospital, which allowed him to continue his studies in theology. During his time as a student, he also served as both vice-chairman and chairman of the Uppsala Student Union. In 1894, he married Anna Söderblom, née Forsell. The couple had 12 children together.

=== Religious Studies and Professorship === Söderblom had a broad range of interests within religious studies. He focused on missions and practical theology, but he was also very curious about Iranian religion. He began studying the Zoroastrian text, the Avesta, which may have contributed to his move to Paris in 1894, where there were much better opportunities for deeper study. He took up a position as pastor of the Swedish community there, which included many Swedish artists, and he also served as a chaplain in Calais and Dunkerque. In 1895, Söderblom collected funds at the request of others to enable August Strindberg to receive hospital care. He continued his studies alongside this work, at the École pratique des Hautes Études, where he learned Persian languages, especially Avestan, and continued his study of the Avesta.

As a side note from his time in Paris, it is worth mentioning that Alfred Nobel consulted Söderblom about his ideas for a prize and about his play, Nemesis, which was published shortly after Nobel's death. Anna Söderblom was concerned that the dramatic and daring content would give a wrong impression of Nobel, and in consultation with the estate, they destroyed almost all copies of the play after Nobel's death in 1896, leaving only three books intact.

In 1901, he obtained his doctorate from the Sorbonne with a thesis on Zoroastrianism, "La Vie Future D'Après Le Mazdéisme," which attracted international attention. The same year, he was appointed professor of theological principles and theological encyclopedia at Uppsala University, and in addition to this position, he served as pastor of the Holy Trinity parish in Uppsala. In 1912, he also became a professor of religious history at the University of Leipzig, in Germany. There, he was particularly admired for his studies of Iranian religion and his writings on Protestantism. In 1914, he published his collected views on his historical research into religions in the book "Gudstrons uppkomst." His studies of Christianity are expressed, among other things, in the writings "Uppenbarelsereligion" (1903/1930) and "Religionsproblemet inom katolicism och protestantism" (in two volumes, 1910), and he also devoted himself to research on Martin Luther's views on religion.

As a professor in 1908, he participated in the founding of the Olaus Petri Foundation, which was affiliated with Uppsala University, and remained its executive director for life. Its purpose was to support theological studies and lectures with an international scope. Later, he began an ambitious series of lectures in Edinburgh, "The Gifford Lectures" (Gifford Lectures) in 1931, but he only completed the first part, which was posthumously published in Swedish in 1933 under the title "Den levande Guden."

=== Archbishop and Peace Work === Following a drawing of lots within one of the electoral bodies, and during the tenure of Minister for Ecclesiastical Affairs Karl Gustaf Westman, Söderblom was appointed archbishop on May 20, 1914. With this appointment, he also became the pro-chancellor of Uppsala University and the pastor of Bälinge parish in the Diocese of Uppsala. The deciding factor in his appointment was not his theological or political stances, but rather that the government wanted a pro-chancellor who would not create conflict with the university. In the selection process, they followed the wishes of the professors at the university. He was consecrated as archbishop on November 8, 1914 (which marked the first time since 1670 that an archiepiscopal consecration took place; all previous archbishops had already been bishops when appointed). He held the office until his death in 1931.

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts, but rather interpretations and evaluations, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Nathan Söderblom was a Swedish church leader and scholar who became Archbishop in 1914. He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1930 for his work promoting peace between different religions.
lattlast:
Nathan Söderblom was born in Hälsingland in 1866 and studied at Uppsala University. He became a priest after studying theology, which is the study of God, and later worked as a researcher and professor. He was very interested in different religions and wrote several books on the subject.

During his time in Paris, he worked with both languages and religion. There, he also met Alfred Nobel, who asked him for ideas for a prize. Söderblom later became a professor at Uppsala University and helped to establish a foundation to support studies in theology.

In 1914, he was appointed Archbishop, which is the highest leadership position in the Church of Sweden. He held this important position until his death in 1931. He also became a member of the Swedish Academy and received the Nobel Peace Prize for his work promoting understanding between different religious beliefs.
